draft.introduction: Exodus 14 recounts the crossing of the Red Sea, Israel’s foundational salvation event. God parts the waters, the people pass through on dry ground, and the pursuing Egyptian army is destroyed. From the Septuagint.

translation.text: And Moses stretched out his hand over the sea, and the Lord drove the sea back by a strong east wind all night and made the sea dry land, and the water was divided. And the children of Israel went into the midst of the sea on the dry ground, and the water was a wall to them on the right and on the left.
translation.original_text: ἐξέτεινεν δὲ Μωυσῆς τὴν χεῖρα ἐπὶ τὴν θάλασσαν

translation.text: So the Lord saved Israel that day from the hand of the Egyptians, and Israel saw the Egyptians dead on the seashore. And Israel saw the great work that the Lord did against the Egyptians, and the people feared the Lord, and they believed God and Moses his servant.
translation.original_text: καὶ ἐρρύσατο κύριος τὸν Ισραηλ ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ ἐκείνῃ

translator_notes.note: The crossing through water became the primary typological image for baptism in the early church (1 Corinthians 10:1–2). The passage from slavery through water to freedom mirrors the baptismal passage from sin through water to new life. The patristic baptismal liturgies drew on this imagery extensively.
